narrative:
Let's take a moment to understand the prediction made by the model regarding your bar exam outcome. Currently, the model predicts that you might not pass the bar exam on your first attempt, which has led to the rejection of your university application. This prediction is based on several factors related to your academic performance, family background, and other personal attributes.

To help you understand what could change this prediction, we use something called counterfactual scenarios. These are "what if" situations that show how altering certain aspects of your profile could lead to a different outcome, specifically passing the bar exam and having your application approved.

In your case, the model identified a few key areas that, if improved, could change the prediction. Notably, your undergraduate GPA (ugpa) and whether you work full-time or part-time (fulltime) are significant factors. In the scenarios where the prediction flipped to passing, your GPA was higher than it currently is, and you were working part-time instead of full-time. These changes suggest that improving your academic performance and reducing work commitments could positively impact your chances.

Across the counterfactual scenarios, the most consistent changes involved increasing your GPA and switching to part-time work. These adjustments appeared in multiple scenarios, indicating their importance. On the other hand, some features, like your gender and racial background, remained unchanged, suggesting they are less influential in altering the prediction.

In total, several counterfactual scenarios were generated, and the most frequent changes involved your GPA and work status. These features often changed together, highlighting a pattern where academic improvement combined with reduced work hours could be an effective strategy.

Based on these insights, you might consider focusing on boosting your GPA, perhaps by seeking additional academic support or resources. Additionally, if feasible, reducing your work hours to part-time could allow more time for study and preparation, potentially improving your exam performance. While these changes may require effort and adjustment, they represent realistic steps you can take to enhance your chances of passing the bar exam and securing your university admission.
